item.title: 🔥 Why Everyone Is Sleeping On TinyGo: Run Go on Microcontrollers and the Web (WASM) Today!
item.author: Yevhen Kozachenko 🇺🇦
item.content:encoded:
What if I told you that your Go programs could run not only on Linux servers but inside a browser or on a microcontroller smaller than your thumb? Welcome to the world of TinyGo 👾.When you hear "Go," you probably think cloud servers, APIs, or blazing-fast concurrency. But there’s a lesser-known sibling in the Go ecosystem—and it packs a serious punch in the embedded and WebAssembly (WASM) space. It's called TinyGo, and it's about to blow your dev hat off 🎩💨.In this post, we’re diving deep into:What TinyGo is and why it matters.Real pain points it solves for embedders and web devs.Running Go on an Arduino.Compiling Go to WASM that’s smaller, faster, and install-free.Code examples and tips to get you started.TinyGo is a Go compiler created for resource-constrained environments. While the official Go compiler (gc) is focused on desktop/server environments, TinyGo is designed for microcontrollers and even WebAssembly targets.Write Go code for microcontrollers like Arduino, BBC micro:bit, Raspberry Pi Pico, etc.Compile Go into WASM that’s small enough for frontend production use.Share code across embedded and web-based applications (yes, actual code reuse).
😬 Real-World Pain Points It Solves
Pain #1: Embedded Devs Are Still in 1998 If you’ve done embedded dev, you're familiar with this:30 minutes to debug a missing semicolonToolchains from the Windows XP era 🤢Modern tooling (VSCode, go fmt, etc.)Concurrency via goroutines (yes, it works in TinyGo!)
Pain #2: WebAssembly Builds Are Thick Go WebAssembly files via the official compiler can be as large as 2MB+. That’s a non-starter for client-side app performance.TinyGo generates WASM binaries often under 500 KB, sometimes as little as 100 KB depending on the app. That’s a 4–20x size reduction ⏬
🦾 Code Example: Blink an LED Using Go 🎇 Let’s run a Go program on an Arduino (UNO, Nano, or whatever you're using).brew tap tinygo-org/tools brew tinygo Install avrdude for flashing Arduino: Here’s our example main.go:
🔥 Compile and Flash to Your Microcontroller tinygo flash arduino main.go Boom—your LED should now be blinking at half-second intervals, using Go.
🕸️ Code Example: WebAssembly With Go + TinyGo Here's a minimal WASM app in Go that runs in the browser:tinygo build add.wasm wasm main.go
HTML Interface: index.html WASM Add in GoAdd 3 + 4Open in any browser—no server needed. Just serve via a static file server.Official Go WASM: ~2–4 MB✅ This is production-safe. No more bundling bloated WASM modules.
⚖️ Trade-offs: What’s the Catch? TinyGo doesn’t support the entire Go standard library (yet)Runtime behavior is limited (e.g., GC is simple, no reflection)You may need to refactor traditional Go code to workStill, for many constrained environments or WASM work, these are acceptable trade-offs.
✈️ Reuse Code Between Embedded + Web If you’re building, say, an IoT device and a dashboard:Write computation logic in pure GoCompile one version for the device (embedded)Compile to WASM for the browserImport into your embedded firmware and also into your WASM frontend.That’s code reuse between hardware and frontend, in Go.
🧠 Final Thoughts: Why TinyGo Is the Hidden Gem Embedded development feels ancientWebAssembly is gaining traction but has usability barriersTinyGo is slicing through the noise with a modern, minimal, and cross-domain approach.Whether you're an embedded dev tired of C/C++ or a frontend junkie exploring WASM, give TinyGo a weekend. You'll be amazed.Let me know what you build with it! 🚀If you liked this post, share it or tweet me at @yourhandle. Want a tutorial on TinyGo with Bluetooth or sensor integration? Let me know 👇🛠️ If you need help building production-ready applications in TinyGo or shipping WASM-based features to the frontend — we offer such services.

item.title: Sponge: A Framework That Reshapes the Go Development Experience and Supercharges Your Projects
item.author: gvison
item.content:encoded:
Go is simple, efficient, and outstanding in concurrency performance—a true breath of fresh air in backend development. We love its purity and its “less is more” philosophy. But let’s be honest: this “purity” sometimes means starting from scratch, reinventing the wheel every single time.Every day you’re writing similar , handling endless database CRUD operations, manually binding data and registering services for HTTP and gRPC… Feels draining, right? Project iterations fly by, requirements pile up, yet you’re still stuck with headaches about project structure, dependency management, logging, and configuration.It’s like wanting to drive an F1 car, but instead spending all day hand-assembling the tires.That’s the reality of the traditional Go development model. While it offers high flexibility and full control over details, the downsides are clear:: Repetitive tasks slow down the pace of core business logic.: Beyond business needs, you also have to handle engineering, maintainability, and scalability issues.: Homemade wheels might “work,” but not necessarily “work well.” Robustness and performance often become concerns.So we can’t help but ask: isn’t there a way to enjoy Go’s high performance while escaping this sea of repetitive work?
Enter the Modern Framework Yes, there is! Just like equipping a hero with legendary gear, modern Go frameworks aim to solve these pain points. They integrate common components and best practices for project development, freeing you to focus on business logic.You might worry: “Isn’t it too heavy?” “Will it restrict my freedom?” “What about performance overhead?”Those are valid concerns. A good framework should act like a , not a boss barking orders. It should deliver:High cohesion, low coupling: Rich functionality, but modular and flexible to use.Convention over configuration: Follow widely accepted practices, reduce decision-making cost, and still keep extensibility.Code generation, productivity boost: Automate repetitive, error-prone work so you can focus on creativity.Which brings us to today’s star: —a “magic tool” dedicated to improving Go development efficiency and experience.
Sponge: Making Go Development as Natural as Breathing is a fascinating open-source project. Its core philosophy is , embracing the low-code approach to free you from manual labor.The coolest part? Like a sponge, it can “absorb” common capabilities and quickly build a stable, reliable, high-performance backend service for you.
One-Click Code Generation: CRUD Is Nothing Picture this: your product manager hands you a prototype with dozens of database tables. Normally, you’d start grumbling while writing DAOs, services, APIs…But in Sponge’s world, all you need is to define your database schema (say, a MySQL DDL), then… click a button!Yes, you read that right. Sponge can generate a complete backend service codebase from your data model, including:HTTP/gRPC service framework codeService/Handler layer codeDAO layer data access codeSwagger (OpenAPI) documentationFor example, with a simple table:Traditionally, you’d have to write a GORM model, create CRUD code, maybe run GORM Gen, then still define API endpoints, register routes, and write handlers—a few hundred lines of code easily.With Sponge, just go to its code generation page, choose your service type ( or ), enter the database DSN, select the table, and click Duang! You instantly get a fully functional, well-structured service. All that’s left is adding your unique into the generated templates.
Microservices? Piece of Cake! Sponge is built with microservices in mind. It comes with service discovery, load balancing, circuit breaking, rate limiting, distributed tracing, and more—all integrated out of the box.Instead of struggling to stitch these features together, Sponge has already paved the way. It decouples backend services into flexible, Lego-like modules, making it effortless to scale from monoliths to complex microservice clusters.
Built-In AI Assistant: Turbocharging Your Coding Here’s the kicker: Sponge includes an AI assistant (supporting DeepSeek, ChatGPT, Gemini, etc.).When you need to implement business logic, just summon the AI—it will help write the actual code and merge it into your project.It’s like strapping a turbo engine onto your development workflow!
Don’t Hesitate—Embrace the Change! From manual gear to automatic, from building your own car to driving a supercar, technology evolves to boost our efficiency and experience.Sticking to “pure native Go” is admirable, but if a powerful framework can help you achieve more with less effort, why not embrace it? Sponge doesn’t take away your control over Go—it raises you higher, so you can focus on meaningful, challenging, and valuable work.Sponge is a framework worth trying. It solves many Go development pain points in a friendly way, letting developers truly “focus only on business logic.”Head over to Sponge’s GitHub repo and try it out for your next project. Trust me, once you experience this “flying” feeling, you’ll never look back.

item.title: Python’s First-Class Functions: a friendly deep dive
item.author: Anik Sikder
item.content:encoded:
If you’ve ever handed someone a tool instead of doing the job yourself, you already get first-class functions. In Python, functions are : you can store them in variables, pass them to other functions, return them, put them in lists and dicts, the whole VIP treatment. Once this clicks, a lot of Python suddenly feels smoother and more powerful.Below is a practical, human-sized tour. We’ll keep the words simple, the examples real, and the pace lively.
What “first-class” really means That’s it. Everything else in this article builds on this idea.
Docstrings & annotations: tiny notes, huge payoffs explain and . hint goes in and out. They don’t change runtime behavior, but they help your future self (and your team, editors, linters, and tests).A good rule: if a function has non-obvious behavior, add a docstring. If a parameter type isn’t obvious, annotate it. You’re processing user input from a CSV. Annotate your converter functions so teammates know what shape to expect.
Lambda expressions: tiny functions on the fly A is a small, one-expression function you define inline.Use lambdas for short glue code, especially with sorting and functional helpers. if the logic needs a name, multiple steps, or a docstring use . remove currency symbols and convert to .
Lambdas and sorting: turning chaos into order Python’s loves a function. Lambdas shine here. For speed and readability when sorting dicts/objects, and (later section) are great.
A playful challenge: “randomize” an iterable using (!!) “Wait, sorted to ?” Yes, as a trick:Each item gets a random key; sorting by those keys yields a random order. this is a cute hack. Prefer for in-place shuffling, it’s faster and clearer. But sometimes you need a non-destructive shuffle (keep the original list unchanged); the trick works nicely.
Function introspection: peeking under the hood Python lets you ask functions about themselves. Build a small CLI or web router that reads annotations and default values to generate help text or form fields automatically.
Callables: not just functions Anything that can be “called” with is a . Functions, methods, lambdas, objects with a method.You can check callability: You can pass around . Great for caching, throttling, validators, and formatters.
Map, filter, zip & list comprehensions These are Python’s little assembly line tools.Apply a function to each item.Keep items where is truthy. with the star operator:Often clearer (and Pythonic) than /: Clean CSV rows.
Reducing functions: folding a sequence into one value applies a function cumulatively. You can write many things with , but in Python we often prefer built-ins (, , , , ) because they’re clearer and faster.Running totals (folding):Better alternatives when they exist: count word frequency (but see note!). That trick is educational, but in real projects use clearer and quicker.
Partial functions: pre-filling arguments for later creates a new function with some arguments fixed. It’s like setting a default once, then reusing it everywhere.Preconfigured validators, loggers, or HTTP calls (e.g., get_json = partial(requests.get, timeout=5)), keeping your code DRY.
The module: tiny, fast, readable helpers provides function versions of Python’s operators and common access patterns. They’re fast and pair beautifully with , , and . for dicts/tuples call a named method They read like English, avoid tiny lambdas, and can be a tad faster.
Patterns you’ll reuse a lot Strategy pattern (choose behavior at runtime):Callbacks for events / hooks: pass functions to be called later (GUI events, web hooks, retries).
Tiny “try it” moments (pick a couple!) : sort a list of student dicts by score desc, then name asc. Use and a negative score or key=lambda s: (-s['score'], s['name']).: make a class that remembers allowed characters and converts titles to URL slugs via .: create round_bd = partial(round, ndigits=0) and use it inside a to tidy numbers from a sensor.First-class functions are not a fancy trick; they’re the heart of “Pythonic” code:They let you like Lego bricks.They keep your code dry, testable, and flexible.They play nicely with the standard library: , , , and friends.What’s a small, elegant function you reach for again and again maybe a tiny formatter, a filter you’re proud of, or a callable class that cleaned up a messy workflow? The best patterns often start small and travel far.

item.title: My Journey with PySpark: Why Every Data Engineer Should Learn It
item.author: Shagun Khandelwal
item.content:encoded:
A few months back, I was staring at a dataset that was way too big for my laptop to handle. I tried the usual Python tricks — Pandas, NumPy — but everything crashed. That’s when I realized: if I want to be a real data engineer, I need something built for scale.That’s when I stumbled upon PySpark. 🚀At first, I thought Spark was just another “buzzword” tool. But soon I learned that PySpark is the Python API for Apache Spark, which means I could use my favorite language (Python) while leveraging Spark’s ability to process massive datasets across clusters.It felt like magic ✨ — suddenly, I wasn’t limited by my machine’s memory anymore.⚡ Why PySpark Changed the Game for MeWhen I compared it to the old-school Hadoop MapReduce, the difference was night and day:MapReduce kept writing things to disk → super slow.Spark keeps most things in memory → blazing fast.With PySpark, I could write simple, readable Python code, not 100 lines of complex MapReduce jobs.That’s when I understood why companies like Netflix, Amazon, and Uber rely on it. If you’re a data engineer dealing with terabytes of data, PySpark feels less like a tool and more like a superpower. 💪🧑‍💻 My First PySpark CodeI still remember the first time I ran this:from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
# Start Spark spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("FirstPySparkApp").getOrCreate()
# Sample data data = [("Alice", 34), ("Bob", 45), ("Cathy", 29)] columns = ["Name", "Age"]
# Create DataFrame df = spark.createDataFrame(data, columns)
# Show Data df.show()
# Simple transformation df.filter(df.Age > 30).show()
And the output? A neat little table printed out in my terminal. Nothing fancy — but the realization that the same code could scale to billions of rows on a cluster… that gave me goosebumps.🚀 Why You Should Learn PySpark TooIf you’re someone who dreams of building big data pipelines, cloud solutions, or ML workflows at scale, PySpark is your best friend.It’s widely used in the industry.It makes you stand out in data engineering interviews.And honestly… it’s just fun to see huge datasets bend to your commands. 😎So if you haven’t already, start experimenting with PySpark today. Trust me — your future self (and your resume) will thank you.

item.title: 🚀 New Series on Data Engineering Tools & Platforms
item.author: Shagun Khandelwal
item.content:encoded: I’m Shagun Khandelwal, currently working as a Big Data Engineer at Infosys with 3 years of experience in the data engineering space. Over the years, I’ve had the opportunity to work with large-scale data pipelines, distributed systems, and modern platforms that make handling terabytes of data efficient and impactful.💡 Data engineering is the backbone of modern data-driven companies. While Data Science and AI get most of the spotlight, it’s the data engineering layer that ensures data is accessible, reliable, and scalable.That’s why I’ve decided to start a series where I’ll be sharing my learnings, practical tips, and deep dives into different tools, frameworks, and platforms that every data engineer should know.What you can expect in this series:🔹 Basics of Data Engineering & its role in modern organizations🔹 Hands-on insights into tools like PySpark, Kafka, Airflow, Hadoop, Databricks, and Cloud Platforms (Azure/GCP/AWS)🔹 Best practices for scalability, optimization, and performance tuning🔹 Real-world use cases from my professional experienceThis series is meant for aspiring data engineers, data enthusiasts, and professionals who want to get practical knowledge of how things work in real-world projects.Stay tuned — the first post will be dropping soon! Follow me here so you don’t miss out 🙂Let’s learn and grow together! 🌱

His flagship Python Bootcamp is a top-rated bestseller with over 1.5 million students enrolled.What makes Jose's teaching stand out is how he breaks down complex topics into manageable, engaging lessons.He combines theory with hands-on exercises, quizzes, and mini-projects to help you internalize concepts.Whether you're a total beginner or brushing up on fundamentals, Jose's clear explanations and practical teaching style make learning Python both fun and effective.Colt Steele is known for creating comprehensive beginner-friendly courses, and his Python Bootcamp is no exception.If you're intimidated by coding, Colt's approach will put you at ease.He uses analogies, visuals, and step-by-step instructions to make even the most technical concepts accessible.What sets his course apart is the , clean coding, and real-world tools like Git, virtual environments, and testing. It's a perfect starter course if you're transitioning into software development.If you want to build practical applications while learning Python, Ardit Sulce's Python Mega Course is the way to go.He takes you beyond just the basics by walking you through 20 projects, including web apps, data dashboards, web scrapers, and more.Ardit's background in data science shows in the course's structure --- he emphasizes how to use Python to solve real problems.His content is ideal for intermediate learners looking to sharpen their skills in web development and automation.Tim Buchalka brings decades of real-world experience into his teaching.His Python Programming Masterclass covers not just syntax and logic but also dives into topics like object-oriented programming, error handling, and best practices for writing production-ready code.What makes Tim's course valuable is the focus on software engineering principles --- perfect for those who want to transition into full-time programming roles.He also keeps his courses well-maintained and updated with new features and tools.
Python Cheat Sheet for Beginners And here is the Python cheat sheet I was talking about. Beginners can use this to learn key Python concepts quicklyThere is no doubt that Udemy offers thousands of Python courses, but the instructor you choose can make all the difference. The five folks have collectively taught the Python programming language to more than 5 million people, and they are among the most respected, experienced, and effective Python teachers on the platform.Whether you're a complete beginner or an aspiring developer looking to level up, you'll find their courses engaging, practical, and career-focused.Take some time to watch the previews, explore the course curricula, and pick the one that fits your learning style. With dedication and the right instructor, you'll be well on your way to mastering Python in 2025.By the way, if you want to join multiple courses on Udemy, it may be worth getting a , which will give instant access to more than 11,000 top-quality Udemy courses for just $30 a month.If you have a lot of time and want to save money, the Udemy Personal Plan will be perfect for you.All the best with your Python journey !!

item.title: Show HN: Clearcam – Add AI object detection to your IP CCTV cameras

item.content:encoded: This runs YOLOv8 + bytetrack with Tinygrad detections (depending on user config) are saved and can be sent to the companion iOS app along with a notification, all video processing is done locally, all footage is encrypted before leaving your computer, and the sending notifications + videos part is optional. item.title: Django Registration,Login Logout and Profile Easy Setup
item.author: Suman

item.title: Django Registration,Login Logout and Profile Easy Setup
item.author: Suman
item.content:encoded:
-- to seperate the logic of registeration,login and logout and create a module that can be integrated in any Django applicationfrom django.urls import path from .import views urlpatterns = [ path('sign_up/', views.index, name='users-sign-up') ] Create forms.py in User app and write the following:from django.contrib.auth.forms import UserCreationForm from django import forms from django.contrib.auth.models import User class SignUpForm(UserCreationForm): email = forms.EmailField(required=True) class Meta: model = User fields = ['username','email','password1','password2'] def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs): super(SignUpForm, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs) for fieldname in ['password1','password2']: self.fields[fieldname].help_text = None from django.shortcuts import render,redirect from .forms import SignUpForm def index(request): if request.method == "POST": form = SignUpForm(request.POST) if form.is_valid(): form.save() return redirect('login') else: form = SignUpForm() context = { 'form':form } return render(request,'User/sign_up.html',context) {% block title %}Sign Up{% endblock %}
{% csrf_token %} {{ form|crispy }}
Add this to base.html to show title dynamically:{% block title %}{% endblock %} from django.contrib.auth import views as auth_view path('login/', auth_view.LoginView.as_view(template_name='User/login.html'), name='login'), {% block title %}Sign In{% endblock %}
{% csrf_token %} {{ form|crispy }}
LOGIN_REDIRECT_URL = 'home'
    {% if request.user.is_authenticated %}
  • New BlogPost
  • Logout
    • {% else %}
  • Login
  • Register
    • {% endif %}
path('logout/', views.LogoutView.as_view(next_page='home'), name='logout'), (only for the flow of execution){% block title %}Logout{% endblock %}
{% csrf_token %} {{ form|crispy }}
You have been logged out.
Use this url template tag wherever required in navbar:from django.contrib.auth.forms import UserCreationForm from django import forms from django.contrib.auth.models import User from .models import Profile class SignUpForm(UserCreationForm): email = forms.EmailField(required=True) class Meta: model = User fields = ['username','email','password1','password2'] class EditProfile(forms.ModelForm): class Meta: model = User fields = ['username','email'] def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs): super(EditProfile, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs) for fieldname in ['username', 'email']: self.fields[fieldname].help_text = None class EditPro(forms.ModelForm): class Meta: model = Profile fields = ['bio','pic'] widgets = { 'bio': forms.Textarea(attrs={'class': 'form-control', 'rows': 5, 'placeholder': 'Write about yourself...'}), 'pic': forms.ClearableFileInput(attrs={'class': 'form-control'}), } def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs): super(EditPro, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs) for fieldname in ['bio', 'pic']: self.fields[fieldname].help_text = None from django.shortcuts import render,redirect from .forms import SignUpForm,EditProfile,EditPro from django.contrib import messages from django.contrib.auth.views import LogoutView from myBlog.models import UserPost,Category from django.contrib.auth.decorators import login_required # Create your views here. def index(request): if request.method == "POST": form = SignUpForm(request.POST) if form.is_valid(): form.save() return redirect('new_login') else: form = SignUpForm() context = { 'form':form } return render(request,'User/sign_up.html',context) @login_required def userProfile(request): if request.method == "POST": u_form = EditProfile(request.POST or None,instance=request.user) p_form = EditPro(request.POST or None,request.FILES or None,instance=request.user.profile) if u_form.is_valid() and p_form.is_valid(): u_form.save() p_form.save() return redirect('profile') else: u_form = EditProfile(instance=request.user) p_form = EditPro(instance=request.user.profile) blog_count = UserPost.objects.filter(author=request.user).count() category = Category.objects.filter(status = 0) context = { 'u_form':u_form, 'p_form':p_form, 'blog_count':blog_count, 'category':category } return render(request,'User/profile.html',context) path('profile/',views.profile, name='user-profile'), Profile {% extends "base.html" %} {% load crispy_forms_tags %} {% block title %}Profile Page{% endblock %} {% block content %}

from django.db import models from django.contrib.auth.models import User import os,datetime from django.core.validators import FileExtensionValidator class Profile(models.Model): user = models.OneToOneField(User,on_delete=models.CASCADE) pic = models.ImageField(default='default.png',upload_to='avatars/',validators=[FileExtensionValidator(['png','jpg','jpeg','webp'])]) bio = models.TextField(blank=True,null=True) def __str__(self): return self.user.username # settings.py MEDIA_URL = '/media/' MEDIA_ROOT = os.path.join(BASE_DIR, 'media') # urls.py from django.conf import settings from django.conf.urls.static import static if settings.DEBUG: urlpatterns += static(settings.MEDIA_URL, document_root=settings.MEDIA_ROOT) Dump your all Static files for productionSTATIC_URL = '/static/' STATIC_ROOT = BASE_DIR / "staticfiles" python manage.py collectstatic from django.contrib import admin from .models import Profile admin.site.register(Profile)

Also Add default.jpg/png in media folder to show default picUse Django Signals to create profile automatically after user sign upfrom .models import Profile from django.contrib.auth.models import User from django.db.models.signals import post_save from django.dispatch import receiver @receiver(post_save,sender = User) def create_profile(sender,instance,created,*args, **kwargs): if created: Profile.objects.create(user=instance) @receiver(post_save, sender=User) def save_profile(sender, instance, **kwargs): instance.profile.save() from django.apps import AppConfig class UserConfig(AppConfig): default_auto_field = 'django.db.models.BigAutoField' name = 'user' def ready(self): import user.signals That's it :) Happy Coding you all.

item.title: Building Moviezinfo: A Comprehensive Telegram Bot for Movie and TV Series Discovery 🎬
item.author: Yatharth Sanghavi
item.content:encoded:
Ever wished you had a personal movie assistant that could instantly provide detailed information about any film or TV series? Meet – a powerful Telegram bot that transforms how users discover and explore entertainment content.Moviezinfo Bot is a feature-rich Telegram bot built with Python that serves as your ultimate entertainment companion. It integrates with the OMDB API to provide comprehensive information about movies and TV series, complete with intelligent recommendations, trailers, and direct links to watch content.
Smart Search Capabilities : Intelligently determines whether you're searching for a movie or TV series: Get detailed information about specific seasons (e.g., "Money Heist season 1"): Displays cast, director, ratings, genre, release dates, and more
Intelligent Recommendation System : Get personalized recommendations based on your favorite genres: 24-hour cache system for faster recommendation delivery: Discover similar movies and series based on your searches: Automatic content moderation with customizable filtered words: Send announcements to all users and groups simultaneously: Monitor bot usage and user interactions: Daily request limits to prevent quota exhaustion
🛠️ Technical Architecture : Main programming language: Telegram Bot API wrapper: Web framework for webhook handling: Movie and TV series data source: URL shortening serviceThe bot implements an intelligent caching system that stores frequently requested data for up to an hour, significantly reducing API calls and improving response times.Built-in protection against API quota exhaustion with daily request limits and automatic counter resets.
🎯 User Experience Features
Intuitive Search Interface Users can search for content in multiple ways:Direct movie/series name: "Inception"Season-specific: "Money Heist season 1"Each search result includes:High-quality poster imagesCast and crew informationTrailer links via YouTubeWatch links for available contentPersonalized recommendations - Welcome message and bot introduction - Genre-based recommendations - Comprehensive command guide - User profile informationAdministrators can send announcements to all users and groups:Automatic message filtering with customizable keywords:git clone https://github.com/YatharthSanghavi/Movizinfo.git Movizinfo pip requirements.txt python movie_filter_bot.py The bot supports multiple deployment platforms:: Built-in environment variable management: Easy scalability and monitoring: Serverless deployment option
Environment Configuration BOT_TOKEN=your_telegram_bot_token OMDB_API_KEY=your_omdb_api_key MDISK_API_KEY=your_mdisk_api_key DEVELOPER_ID=your_telegram_user_id ENVIRONMENT=production WEBHOOK_URL=https://your-app-url.com/
📊 Performance Optimizations : 1-hour cache for movie/series data: 24-hour cache for genre-based suggestions: Minimized redundant requests: Automatic deletion of bot messages after 80 seconds: Efficient data structures for caching: Robust exception managementSince its deployment, Moviezinfo Bot has:Processed thousands of movie and series queriesProvided intelligent recommendations to entertainment enthusiastsModerated content in multiple Telegram groupsMaintained 99.9% uptime with efficient resource usage: Save movies and series for later viewing: Personal rating system and reviews: Share recommendations with friends: Search by year, rating, duration: Localized content and interface: Persistent user data storage: Improved recommendation algorithms: Detailed usage statistics: Integration with additional movie databasesReady to explore the world of cinema with Moviezinfo Bot?: Search for on Telegram: Follow the setup guide in the README: Submit issues, feature requests, or pull requests
💡 Key Takeaways for Developers Building Moviezinfo Bot taught me several valuable lessons:
API Integration Best Practices Always implement rate limiting and cachingHandle API errors gracefully with fallback mechanismsUse environment variables for sensitive configurationKeep interfaces intuitive and responsiveProvide clear feedback for all user actionsImplement automatic cleanup to reduce chat clutter
Scalability Considerations Design with growth in mind from day oneImplement efficient caching strategiesMonitor and optimize resource usage continuouslyRestrict administrative commands to authorized usersImplement content filtering for group moderationValidate and sanitize all user inputs
🤝 Open Source & Community The entire project is open source! Check it out on GitHub and feel free to:Want to contribute or have questions? 💬 Join the discussion in the issues🔄 Fork and create your own version📧 Reach out with feedbackMoviezinfo Bot demonstrates how modern bot development can create genuinely useful tools that enhance user experiences. By combining smart API integration, intelligent caching, and user-focused design, we've built a platform that makes movie and TV series discovery effortless and enjoyable.Whether you're a movie enthusiast looking for your next binge-watch or a developer interested in building Telegram bots, Moviezinfo Bot offers valuable insights into creating scalable, user-friendly automation tools.What would you add to an Moviezinfo ? Drop your ideas in the comments! 👇If this helped you, consider giving the repo a ⭐ - it means the world to indie developers like me!

item.title: Day 1: Tried building my first CNN (and learned why 8 validation images are useless)
item.author: Jacob
item.content:encoded:
Three days ago, I made my first repo public on GitHub by accident. Panic mode: activated. But instead of hiding it, I decided to share the journey. I’m a final-year medical student diving into the tech side of healthcare, and I just built my first Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to detect pneumonia from chest X-rays. Spoiler: it works… but my data setup was very questionable. Classify chest X-rays as or Python, TensorFlow/Keras, Google Colab, LLMs to support my Simple CNN (~5.4M parameters, 3 conv blocks + pooling + dense head) So sort of, how to tell the left image from the two on the right apart:This was my way of bridging medicine (I see more than 50 shades of grey in an X-ray by now, though I’m not a radiologist) with my growing interest in AI. I didn’t know what a CNN really was at first. For anyone new, this video is a helpful, very basic introduction:For a deeper dive, here’s a great in-depth playlist provided by the channel Futurology - An Optimistic Future:Deep Learning Playlist
1. Tiny validation set = chaos 🎢 The dataset’s “validation” split? Just 8 normal + 8 pneumonia images. That’s 0.14% of the data. Epoch 1 → , Epoch 3 → , Data splitting isn’t just about having folders—it’s about .
2. The lazy pneumonia-predicting machine 🤖 My model started predicting “pneumonia” most of the time, scoring decent accuracy but missing normals. The only purpose of the confusion matrix was to confuse me (ngl, ChatGPT had to interpret it), but it showed the truth: strong on pneumonia, weak on normals. Accuracy ≠ whole story—precision/recall matter.
3. Model summaries aren’t magic 📋 The with intimidated me, until I broke it down: Conv2D → edge/pattern detectors Dense layers → where parameter count explodes (~5.3M of 5.4M) Each “neuron” = input → math → output From a medical lens, it felt like tracing how biological neurons fire.
4. Data quality > fancy architecture 💎 My biggest realization: no clever CNN can fix broken training splits or imbalance. I spent more time fixing the pipeline than tweaking hyperparameters—and that was the right call. Proper 70/15/15 train/val/test splits to balance mistakes Better metrics (precision/recall per class) for underrepresented normal cases The full learning process (project research and notes, confusions, breakthroughs, even “I don’t fully get this yet” moments) is on GitHub:chest-xray-cnnBecause learning isn’t just about polished results—it’s about the messy middle too. I’ll keep sharing as I go—because this is just the start of my journey into AI + healthcare. Happy about any inputs or feedback 🚀 The Kaggle dataset authors for making this resource freely available TensorFlow/Keras docs + tutorials that kept me afloat The open-source community on GitHub ChatGPT (yes, I used it heavily!) for debugging, explaining confusion matrices, emotional support, and not judging my messy code

item.title: 𝐁𝐞𝐲𝐨𝐧𝐝 𝐅𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐡𝐧𝐞𝐬𝐬: 𝐇𝐨𝐰 𝐭𝐨 𝐮𝐬𝐞 𝐒𝐞𝐚𝐫𝐜𝐡 𝐌𝐨𝐝𝐞𝐬 𝐢𝐧 𝐕𝐞𝐜𝐭𝐨𝐫 𝐃𝐚𝐭𝐚𝐛𝐚𝐬𝐞𝐬 (𝐰𝐢𝐭𝐡 𝐋𝐚𝐧𝐠𝐜𝐡𝐚𝐢𝐧)
item.author: RajeevaChandra
item.content:encoded:
In my last post, I talked about how dynamic embeddings keep your knowledge base fresh as documents evolve. But freshness is only half the story. When a user asks your assistant a question, 𝐡𝐨𝐰 𝐲𝐨𝐮 𝐬𝐞𝐚𝐫𝐜𝐡 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐯𝐞𝐜𝐭𝐨𝐫 𝐝𝐚𝐭𝐚𝐛𝐚𝐬𝐞 determines whether they get: the single most relevant snippet, a broader set of context, or results filtered by metadata like timestamps or document type. Here are the five main search strategies—explained simply. 1️⃣ Similarity Search (k-NN)When you type a query, the system converts it into a vector Then, it looks around the vector space for the “neighbors” that sit closest. Those become your top results👉 Example: Query: “What is the required capital reserve?” Result: “Banks must maintain 12% capital reserves.”2️⃣ Max Marginal Relevance (MMR)MMR makes sure you don’t get the same answer five times in a row. Here’s how it works: after finding the most relevant snippet, it deliberately looks for other results that are still relevant but it balances relevance with diversity.👉 Example: Query: “Explain capital reserve requirements.” Results: “Banks must maintain 12% capital reserves.” “These reserves are adjusted annually based on regulations.”Notice how the second snippet doesn’t just repeat the first—it brings in a new angle. That’s MMR at work.3️⃣ Filtered / Metadata Search Sometimes “closest meaning” isn’t the whole story—you also care about context and constraints. That’s where metadata filtering comes in. Think of it as adding a funnel on top of similarity search. You still find the closest matches, but only those that meet extra rules like date, document type, source, or author.👉 Example: Query: “What’s the latest capital reserve requirement?” Filter: updated_at > 2025-01-01 Result: The system ignores older documents and only shows the most recent rule—even if the older ones are technically “closer” in meaning.4️⃣ Hybrid Search (Keyword + Vector)Sometimes, meaning alone isn’t enough. What if your query includes an exact code, acronym, or ID? A pure semantic search might blur it, but a keyword search nails it.Hybrid search combines the two:Vector search captures the context and meaning. Keyword search makes sure specific terms (like “CRR-2025”) get the priority they deserve.Query: “Capital Reserve Rule CRR-2025” Vector search → understands it’s about capital reserves. Keyword search → ensures documents mentioning CRR-2025 are ranked higher.5️⃣ Cross-Encoder RerankingStarts with a fast similarity search, then uses a deeper model (like BERT) to re-score the top candidates for accuracy.👉 Query: “What are the capital reserve rules for 2025?” Step 1: Initial retrieval → 10 candidates Step 2: Reranker → re-scores and picks the single best snippet
📊 Search Strategies at a Glance Finds nearest neighbors in vector spaceCan return repetitive or narrow resultsMax Marginal Relevance (MMR)Balances relevance + diversityAvoids duplicates, adds varietyExplanations, multi-fact answersFiltered / Metadata SearchAdds constraints (date, type, source) on top of similarityEnsures results match business rulesNeeds clean, consistent metadataCompliance, regulations, versioned docsCombines keyword search with vector similarityBest of both worlds (context + exact match)Requires extra infra (ElasticSearch, OpenSearch)IDs, codes, acronyms, technical docsRe-scores initial candidates with a deeper model (e.g., BERT)Mission-critical answers, high-accuracy appsStatic embeddings = stale snapshotsDynamic embeddings = living knowledgeThis pipeline keeps context fresh and supports multiple retrieval modes so you can choose the right strategy for your production needs.

item.title: Fighting AI Bias: An Application That Makes Ethics Interactive
item.author: Siddles835
item.content:encoded: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is everywhere — from TikTok feeds to job applications. But here’s the problem: AI isn’t always fair. If an algorithm is trained on biased data, it can treat people unfairly, even if isn't intended to do so. Hence, I built an application that makes this problem visible, interactive, and solvable.My app, built entirely in Python with Streamlit, lets you:Type in a sentence and see how an AI sentiment model reacts.Detects gendered or racial words.Shows a “bias score” visualization.Explore Image Bias (Simulated)The app runs a classifier to show how bias can creep in.Visual charts reveal how certain groups might be treated differently.Try debiasing AI yourself.Adjust sliders to re-weight how much the model values fairness vs. accuracy.See live how the bias gap shrinks.This way, the app isn’t just a demo — it’s a sandbox where anyone can play, learn, and even try to fix the bias themselves.Streamlit for the Interactive UITransformers (Hugging Face) for text sentiment analysis.Matplotlib/Seaborn for data visualizations.A modular project structure with multiple demos inside one app.AI Bias is not a future problem - it's happening now.Technology should be fair, let's keep it that way.

item.title: What is Backpropagation?
item.author: Dev Patel
item.content:encoded:
Imagine teaching a dog a new trick. You show them, reward correct attempts, and correct mistakes. This iterative process of learning from feedback is the essence of backpropagation in neural networks. This article will demystify this crucial concept, revealing how neural networks learn and adapt, powering everything from self-driving cars to medical diagnosis.Backpropagation, short for "backward propagation of errors," is the core algorithm that allows neural networks to learn from data. It's the engine that drives the network's ability to adjust its internal parameters (weights and biases) to minimize prediction errors. Essentially, it's a sophisticated form of trial-and-error, guided by mathematics.
Understanding the Neural Network Landscape Before diving into backpropagation, let's quickly visualize a simple neural network. Imagine a network with an input layer (receiving data), a hidden layer (processing information), and an output layer (making predictions). Each connection between neurons has an associated weight, representing the strength of that connection, and each neuron has a bias, influencing its activation.
The Core Mechanics: Calculating the Error and Adjusting Weights Backpropagation works in two phases: The input data flows through the network, layer by layer, until a prediction is made at the output layer. This prediction is then compared to the actual target value, calculating the error. This is where the magic happens. The error is propagated backward through the network, layer by layer. For each weight and bias, the algorithm calculates how much it contributed to the overall error (using a concept called the gradient). This gradient indicates the direction of steepest descent in the error landscape. The weights and biases are then adjusted proportionally to the gradient, reducing the error.
The Math Behind the Magic: Gradient Descent The heart of backpropagation is gradient descent. Imagine a hilly landscape where the height represents the error. Our goal is to find the lowest point (minimum error). Gradient descent works by taking small steps downhill, following the negative gradient.The gradient is calculated using the chain rule of calculus. While the full derivation can be complex, the core idea is straightforward: A common error function is the Mean Squared Error (MSE): MSE = 1/n * Σ(y_i - ŷ_i)², where is the actual value and is the predicted value. This involves calculating the partial derivative of the error function with respect to each weight and bias. This tells us how much a small change in each weight or bias would affect the error.Update the weights and biases: The weights and biases are updated using the following formula: w_new = w_old - learning_rate * ∂E/∂w, where controls the step size.
Illustrative Python Pseudo-code Here's a simplified representation of the weight update process:
Real-World Applications: Where Backpropagation Shines Backpropagation is the backbone of countless applications: Powering image classification systems like those used in self-driving cars and facial recognition.Natural Language Processing: Enabling machine translation, sentiment analysis, and chatbots. Assisting doctors in diagnosing diseases from medical images and patient data. Predicting stock prices and assessing financial risk.
Challenges and Limitations While powerful, backpropagation has limitations: The algorithm might get stuck in a local minimum, a point that seems like the lowest point but isn't the global minimum.Vanishing/Exploding Gradients: In deep networks, gradients can become very small (vanishing) or very large (exploding) during backpropagation, hindering learning. Training large neural networks can be computationally expensive, requiring significant processing power.The widespread use of backpropagation-based neural networks raises ethical concerns: If the training data is biased, the resulting model will likely be biased, leading to unfair or discriminatory outcomes.Transparency and Explainability: Understanding why a neural network makes a particular prediction can be challenging, raising concerns about accountability.
The Future of Backpropagation Backpropagation remains a cornerstone of deep learning, but ongoing research aims to improve its efficiency and address its limitations. New optimization algorithms, architectural innovations (like residual networks), and techniques for improving model interpretability are actively being developed, promising even more powerful and reliable neural networks in the future. The journey into understanding backpropagation is not just about mastering an algorithm; it's about understanding the fundamental principles that power the artificial intelligence revolution.

item.title: BinGo: Gamificando a Reciclagem de Lixo Eletrônico e Construindo o Futuro Sustentável nas Escolas!
item.author: Marco Caparra
item.content:encoded: Um projeto inovador que une tecnologia, educação e sustentabilidade para transformar o descarte de e-waste em uma jornada recompensadora.*O Desafio Invisível: Lixo Eletrônico e a Urgência da Reciclagem * Você já parou para pensar na quantidade de celulares, notebooks, fones de ouvido e outros aparelhos eletrônicos que descartamos todos os dias? O lixo eletrônico (e-waste) é um dos resíduos que mais crescem no mundo e representa uma ameaça ambiental significativa. Ele contém substâncias tóxicas que contaminam solo e água, mas também materiais valiosos que podem ser recuperados.Apesar da importância, a conscientização e a infraestrutura para o descarte adequado ainda são limitadas, especialmente em ambientes educacionais. Como podemos engajar as novas gerações nessa causa vital?É aqui que o Projeto BinGo entra em cena!*BinGo: Onde a Tecnologia Encontra a Consciência Ambiental * O BinGo é mais do que um projeto; é uma solução gamificada robusta, concebida para revolucionar o descarte de lixo eletrônico em escolas. Nossa missão é transformar um hábito muitas vezes ignorado em uma experiência divertida, educativa e, acima de tudo, recompensadora para alunos do Ensino Fundamental e Médio.Como Funciona? A Magia Por Trás do BinGoNosso ecossistema é construído sobre dois pilares inovadores:Lixeiras Inteligentes com Visão Computacional: Esqueça as lixeiras comuns! Nossas "BinGo Bins" são equipadas com tecnologia de visão computacional (Computer Vision). Quando um aluno insere um item eletrônico, a lixeira o reconhece e valida o descarte. Essa validação gera um código único que o aluno utiliza para registrar seu feito.Plataforma Digital Gamificada: Após o descarte, o aluno insere o código único em nossa plataforma online. Automaticamente, ele ganha pontos, sobe no ranking e desbloqueia novos desafios. A plataforma serve como um centro de engajamento, onde os alunos podem: Acompanhar sua pontuação e progresso individual. Ver o ranking de sua turma e da escola. Participar de desafios de reciclagem. E o mais empolgante: trocar seus pontos por recompensas físicas! Pense em brindes, acessos especiais ou até mesmo vantagens dentro da escola.BinGo para Todos: A Experiência MultiusuárioO BinGo é desenhado para engajar toda a comunidade escolar, com interfaces e funcionalidades específicas para diferentes perfis:Alunos: Central para acompanhar pontos, desafios e resgatar recompensas. Professores: Gerenciam suas turmas, acompanham o engajamento individual e coletivo, e podem criar desafios personalizados. Coordenadores/Diretores: Visão geral do desempenho da escola, acompanhamento de métricas de reciclagem e comparação entre turmas. Administradores (da BinGo): Gerenciamento de escolas parceiras, usuários, dados agregados e monitoramento da performance geral da plataforma.*Por Trás da Magia: A Pilha Tecnológica do BinGo * Para construir uma solução tão abrangente e robusta, estamos utilizando um conjunto de tecnologias modernas e eficientes:Backend: O Cérebro da OperaçãoLinguagem: Python, pela sua versatilidade e ecossistema robusto. Framework: FastAPI, escolhido por sua performance, facilidade de uso e documentação automática (Swagger UI/ReDoc), garantindo uma API rápida e bem estruturada. Banco de Dados: MySQL, com gerenciamento de ORM via SQLAlchemy e migrações controladas por Alembic, assegurando a integridade e escalabilidade dos dados. Autenticação: Sistema de autenticação JWT (JSON Web Tokens) para segurança e controle de acesso. Frontend: A Interface da InteraçãoAtualmente, construído com HTML5, CSS3 e JavaScript puro (Vanilla JS) para um controle total e aprendizado. Próximo Passo Ambicioso: Temos planos claros de migrar o frontend para React.js para uma experiência do usuário ainda mais dinâmica, reativa e escalável. Hardware: A Conexão com o Mundo FísicoAs lixeiras inteligentes utilizam microcontroladores como o ESP32 integrados a câmeras para a detecção e validação dos itens de lixo eletrônico através de algoritmos de visão computacional. Nosso Impacto e o Futuro do BinGoO Projeto BinGo não é apenas sobre tecnologia; é sobre educação, conscientização e construção de um futuro mais sustentável. Estamos criando uma ferramenta que empodera jovens a serem agentes de mudança, transformando pequenas ações em um grande impacto ambiental.Estamos em pleno desenvolvimento, com um roadmap ambicioso que inclui a integração completa hardware-software, aprimoramento das funcionalidades de gamificação e o lançamento de pilotos em escolas parceiras. Nossos olhos estão fixos na FECEG, FEMIC e FEBRAT 2025, onde pretendemos apresentar o BinGo em sua plenitude!Quer fazer parte dessa transformação? Seja você um desenvolvedor, educador, entusiasta da sustentabilidade ou apenas alguém que acredita em um mundo melhor, sua participação é muito bem-vinda!Explore nosso Código: Dê uma olhada em como tudo funciona e contribua com o projeto! Nosso repositório é público: ➡️ GitHubSiga Nossa Jornada: Acompanhe as atualizações, novidades e o impacto do BinGo em tempo real. Participe da conversa e ajude a espalhar essa ideia! ➡️ Instagram

item.title: Blockchain Security Expert AI Track
item.author: Insights YRS
item.content:encoded: Blockchain Security Expert AI Track CertiK is a leading company in the blockchain security industry, with a proven track record of success. We have worked with over 4,798 clients, secured over $597 billion worth of digital assets, and monitored over 17,000 projects. Our clients include some of the most prominent projects in the blockchain space, such as Aave, Polygon, Yearn, and Chiliz.We are currently seeking a Blockchain Security Expert to join our AI Track. The primary focus of this role is to develop an intelligent AI agent powered by large language models (LLMs) to enhance our security audit tools in the blockchain space. As a Blockchain Security Expert on the AI Track, you will work closely with our blockchain security team to bridge cutting-edge academic research with practical applications.Your efforts will be dedicated to developing and improving our AI-powered security audit tools, which will help us to identify and mitigate potential security risks in blockchain projects. You will also be responsible for collaborating with our clients to understand their specific security needs and requirements, and for providing them with customized solutions that meet their unique needs.To be successful in this role, you will need to have a strong background in blockchain security, with a particular focus on smart contract auditing and security testing. You should also have experience with large language models and machine learning, and be familiar with the latest developments in these fields.In addition to technical skills, you will need to be a strong communicator, both verbally and in writing. You will be working closely with our clients and our internal team, and will need to be able to explain complex technical concepts in a clear and concise manner.If you are passionate about blockchain security and have a track record of success in this field, we would love to hear from you. We offer a competitive salary and benefits package, as well as opportunities for professional growth and development. If you are interested in this role, please submit your resume and a cover letter outlining your experience and qualifications.

item.title: Title: Microsoft Brings PC Gaming Apps and Stores to Xbox App on Windows
item.author: Insights YRS
item.content:encoded: Title: Microsoft Brings PC Gaming Apps and Stores to Xbox App on Windows Microsoft has announced that it is bringing PC gaming apps and stores to its Xbox app on Windows. This news is a significant development for gamers who have been looking for a more seamless way to access their favorite games and applications on both platforms. In this blog post, we will explore the new features being tested in the Xbox app for Windows 11 and how they will impact the gaming experience for PC users.Microsoft is currently testing a new "My Apps" feature in the Xbox app for Windows 11. This feature allows PC gamers to quickly install and launch third-party apps like browsers, gaming utilities, and even rival storefronts. The "My Apps" feature is designed to make it easier for PC gamers to access their favorite apps and games without having to launch the Xbox app every time.Impact on Gaming ExperienceThe new "My Apps" feature will have a significant impact on the gaming experience for PC users. It will allow them to access their favorite games and applications more quickly and easily, without having to switch between different platforms. This will make it easier for PC gamers to stay up-to-date with the latest games and applications, and will also make it easier for them to find and download new games and applications.Microsoft's decision to bring PC gaming apps and stores to its Xbox app on Windows is a positive development for gamers. The new "My Apps" feature will make it easier for PC gamers to access their favorite games and applications, and will also make it easier for them to find and download new games and applications. This will help to create a more seamless gaming experience for PC users, and will also help to drive growth in the gaming industry.

item.title: Title: Unlocking Value: A Closer Look at Viasat's Defense and Advanced Technologies Business
item.author: Insights YRS
item.content:encoded: Title: Unlocking Value: A Closer Look at Viasat's Defense and Advanced Technologies Business In the fast-paced world of technology, it is always important to keep an eye on the latest developments and trends. One such development that has caught the attention of many investors is the growing interest in defense and advanced technologies. In this blog post, we will take a closer look at Viasat's Defense and Advanced Technologies business and explore how this hidden gem may be unlocking value for the company.The Crown Jewel: Viasat's Defense and Advanced Technologies BusinessViasat is a leading global communications company that provides innovative solutions for a variety of industries, including defense and advanced technologies. This business segment is often referred to as the "crown jewel" of Viasat, as it has shown significant growth and potential for further expansion.The Defense and Advanced Technologies business is focused on providing cutting-edge solutions for military and government customers. This includes a range of products and services, such as satellite communications, cybersecurity, and intelligence, surveillance, and reconnaissance (ISR) solutions. The business has a strong track record of delivering high-quality products and services to its customers, and it has shown significant growth in recent years.Potential for a Spinoff or IPOGiven the strength and potential of Viasat's Defense and Advanced Technologies business, it is no surprise that activist investor Carronade has taken notice. The investor has dubbed this business segment a "hidden gem" that may be due for a spinoff or IPO.A spinoff or IPO could provide several benefits for Viasat and its shareholders. For example, it could allow the company to focus more resources on its core business segments, while also providing shareholders with the opportunity to invest in a separate, high-growth company. Additionally, a spinoff or IPO could help to unlock the full value of Viasat's Defense and Advanced Technologies business, as it could attract new investors and increase the company's overall market capitalization.In conclusion, Viasat's Defense and Advanced Technologies business is a hidden gem that has shown significant growth and potential for further expansion. Activist investor Carronade has taken notice of this business segment and has dubbed it a "crown jewel" that may be due for a spinoff or IPO. While the future of this business remains to be seen, it is clear that Viasat and its shareholders stand to benefit from unlocking the full value of this high-growth segment.

item.title: 10 Python Tips I Wish I Knew as a Beginner 🐍
item.author: Chimezie Nwankwo
item.content:encoded:
When I first started learning Python, I wasted hours trying to figure out things that now feel . If you’re just starting your Python journey, these tips will save you time, confusion, and frustration.
Here are 10 Python tips I wish I knew as a beginner 👇 Debugging? Don’t just print variables randomly. to format your output: python name = "Alice" age = 20 print(f"My name is {name}, and I am {age} years old.") --- 2. Learn List Comprehensions Early
Instead of writing long loops: squares = [] for i in range(5): squares.append(i * i) DO THIS : squares = [i * i for i in range(5)]
Cleaner, shorter, more Pythonic. --- 3. The Power of enumerate()
Instead of: i = 0 for item in ["a", "b", "c"]: print(i, item) i += 1 USE: for i, item in enumerate(["a", "b", "c"]): print(i, item) --- 4. Use zip() for Parallel Iteration names = ["Alice", "Bob", "Charlie"] scores = [90, 85, 92]
for name, score in zip(names, scores): print(f"{name}: {score}") --- 5. Master Slicing
Python lets you grab parts of a list easily: nums = [0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5] print(nums[1:4]) # [1, 2, 3] print(nums[:3]) # [0, 1, 2] print(nums[-2:]) # [4, 5]
To be continued....

item.content:encoded: 🚀 LeetCode 3197: Covering All Ones with 3 Rectangles (C++, Python & Java)🚀

item.title: 🚀 LeetCode 3197: Covering All Ones with 3 Rectangles (C++, Python & Java)🚀
item.author: Om Shree
item.content:encoded:
LeetCode’s is one of those grid-based problems that force us to think deeply about partitioning, optimization, and dynamic computation. Many developers struggle with finding efficient solutions because it requires geometry + DP + prefix sums reasoning.Problem Statement (LeetCode 3197)We are given a . We need to find 3 non-overlapping rectangles (aligned with grid axes, non-zero area, allowed to touch) such that and the sum of areas is minimized.Input: grid = [[1,0,1], [1,1,1]]
Output: 5 Cover and → rectangle of area 2Cover and → rectangle of area 2Cover alone → rectangle of area 1 To cover all s in a region with one rectangle, we can just compute the smallest bounding rectangle of the 1’s. To cover with , we try partitioning by row or column.Dynamic Programming + Prefix Sums:Use row and column suffix sums to quickly check presence of s in a region. → Minimum area with → Minimum area with Finally combine for using partitions.This hierarchical breakdown (1 → 2 → 3 rectangles) avoids brute force and gives a much faster solution.
🧩 Step-by-Step Solution Strategy : Number of s in row from column to end.: Number of s in column from row to bottom.Shrinks boundaries until we enclose all s → returns area of bounding box.Try splitting the region into 2 parts (horizontal or vertical).Minimize sum of for each partition.For 3 rectangles, try splitting first into or partitions.Iterate over .
🖥️ C++ Implementation (Efficient Solution) Here’s the solution you shared, polished for readability:Each : after shrinking boundariesEach : (splitting rows + cols)Final loops: partitions✅ Overall complexity: (efficient for )This problem is a great example of combining geometry + DP partitioning + prefix sums.It teaches us how to reduce a seemingly exponential partition problem into manageable recursive functions (, , and final combo).Having multiple language implementations makes the logic accessible to more developers.

item.title: 🚀 FastAPI vs Rails — Notes from a Rails Developer Learning FastAPI
item.author: Santanu Bhattacharya
item.content:encoded:
I recently explored FastAPI alongside my usual Rails work and built a small project to learn the essentials:✅ CRUD APIs (with items) ✅ User authentication with JWT (login & registration) ✅ Integrated a Weather API wrapper ✅ DB operation, I use sqlite3 ✅ Hands-on with Alembic migrations & SQLModel ✅ Password hashing ✅ Handling Environment variables and a few more things...The learning curve was smooth, and I like how FastAPI makes API development clean. 🚀Would love feedback and ideas on what to add next!
To help myself memorize and compare, I drafted this write-up. Hopefully, it helps anyone else coming from Rails into FastAPI. I am planning to explore for Background jobs anf file upload. I’ll keep updating this as I learn more. If you’re also coming from Rails, maybe this cheat sheet will help!See my code in the above shared gihub link. Thanks!!!

item.title: Waitgroups: what they are, how to use them and what changed with Go 1.25'
item.author: Matheus Mina
item.content:encoded: Imagine the following problem: you need to process hundreds of records and generate a single output. One way to solve this is to process each record sequentially and unify the output only at the end. However, this can be extremely slow, depending on the time spent processing each record. Another way is to process them concurrently, speeding up the overall time. In my post about introduction to concurrency, I talked a bit about and . Now, I've decided to talk about , a way to simplify the management of multiple . are part of the sync package, and their use is relatively simple. For each you start, you must add 1 to the counter, and then you have to wait for all to finish their work. Each must reduce the counter by 1 to indicate its completion. For example:Although they are easy to use, you must always ensure the correct number of in the . In other words, for every added with , you must have a . If this doesn't happen, it can cause a deadlock during . This occurs, for example, if we add a to the and never call . The reverse of this problem is finishing more than were added to the waitgroup, which generates a with the message panic: sync: negative WaitGroup counter. However, this problem is intermittent, as the main process might finish before the . To avoid these cases, the goleak library implements a leak validator.Starting with Go version 1.25, everything changed, and our API became even simpler and free of these problems! Instead of manually having to control which were added and signal their end, we can simply use the new function , which does this automatically.Since this function accepts a as an argument, you need to wrap your function inside another one if you need to pass arguments. As the increment and decrement are now handled automatically, the problems mentioned above no longer exist, and it has indeed become even simpler to work concurrently.Although it is not the focus of this post, I want to mention another improvement in this version of the language. Before version 1.25, if your application ran on Kubernetes, you needed to use a library like automaxprocs to get a valid CPU value for . Now, this is done for us automatically. For those interested, I recommend reading the article Container-aware GOMAXPROCS.In the next post, I want to explore more about the package and how we can use its other functionalities to manage concurrent work more simply. The examples are in this repository. Comment below what you thought of the post and the new features in Go 1.25!

item.title: Waitgroups: o que são, como usar e o que mudou com o Go 1.25
item.author: Matheus Mina
item.content:encoded: Imagine o seguinte problema: é necessário processar centenas de registros e gerar uma única saída. Uma forma de resolver é processar cada registro sequencialmente e só no fim unificar a saída. Contudo isso pode ser extremamente lento, dependendo do tempo gasto para processar cada registro. Outra forma é fazer o processamento de forma concorrente, acelerando o tempo gasto para o processamento. No meu post sobre introdução à concorrência falei um pouco sobre e e agora decidi falar sobre os , uma forma de simplificar a gestão de múltiplas . são parte do pacote sync e seu uso é relativamente simples. Para cada goroutine disparada, é necessário adicionar 1 ao contador do e depois, deve-se esperar que todas terminem seus trabalhos. É necessário que cada goroutine reduza 1 do contador para indicar seu fim. Exemplificando:Apesar de o uso ser fácil, é necessário se preocupar em sempre ter o número correto de no , ou seja, para cada adicionada com é necessário ter um . Se isso não ocorrer, pode-se causar com um deadlock durante o . Isso acontece, por exemplo, se adicionarmos uma goroutine ao e nunca finalizarmos com o . O inverso deste problema é finalizar mais do que foram adicionadas ao grupo de espera. Neste caso, um é gerado durante a execução do seu programa com a mensagem panic: sync: negative WaitGroup counter. No entanto esse problema é intermitente, pois o processo principal pode finalizar antes da goroutine. Para evitar estes casos, a biblioteca goleak implementa um validador de vazamento de .A partir da versão 1.25 do Go, tudo mudou e nossa API ficou ainda mais simples e sem esses problemas! Em vez de manualmente ser necessário controlar quais foram adicionadas e sinalizar o seu fim, podemos simplesmente utilizar a nova função que já faz isso automaticamente com o .Como essa função recebe um como argumento, é necessário encapsular nossa função dentro de outra, caso seja necessário fornecer argumentos. Como o incremento e decremento passaram a ser feitos de forma automática, os problemas citados acima não existem mais e, de fato, ficou ainda mais simples de se trabalhar de forma concorrente. Apesar de não ser o foco desta postagem, quero citar outra melhoria a partir desta versão da linguagem. Antes da versão 1.25, se sua aplicação executava em Kubernetes, era necessário utilizar alguma biblioteca como automaxprocs para obter um valor de CPU válido para as e agora isso é feito para nós automaticamente. Para quem tiver interesse, recomendo a leitura do artigo Container-aware GOMAXPROCS.No próximo post, quero destrinchar mais sobre o pacote e como podemos utilizar suas outras funcionalidades para gerenciar o trabalho concorrente de forma mais simples. Os exemplos estão neste repositório. Comente abaixo o que achou do post e das novidades do Go 1.25!

item.title: How I Cut AWS RAG System Response Time from 30 Seconds to 100ms
item.author: DEVunderdog
item.content:encoded:
A deep dive into transforming a painful user experience into seamless interactions using resource pooling and state machines
The Pain Point That Changed Everything Picture this: You've just built what you think is an elegant RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation) system using AWS Bedrock, OpenSearch Serverless, and S3. The architecture looks clean on paper. Then you hit the "create knowledge base" button and... wait. And wait. , your users are still staring at a loading spinner.This isn't just poor UX—it's a business killer in today's instant-gratification world.Here's what the AWS documentation doesn't make crystal clear:OpenSearch Serverless indexes take 15-20 seconds to become operationally searchable after creation.This single constraint cascaded into a nightmare of complexity: → 15-20 seconds of mandatory wait timeCreate AWS Knowledge Base → Remote API call with potential failures → Another remote API callConfigure S3 inclusion prefixes → State management complexityHandle failures at any step → Rollback hellEach step could fail independently, creating inconsistent states between our database and AWS resources. The traditional synchronous approach was doomed from the start.
The "Aha" Moment: Think Like a Restaurant The breakthrough came from an unexpected analogy. High-end restaurants don't start cooking your meal when you order—they prep ingredients in advance. They maintain pools of ready-to-use components.What if we pre-provisioned AWS resources and maintained them in pools?
The Solution: Resource Pooling with State Machines I implemented a multi-layered pool system that transforms the user experience:: Click → Wait 30+ seconds → Maybe get a knowledge base: Click → Instant assignment from pre-warmed pool → Seamless experience┌─────────────────┐ ┌─────────────────┐ ┌─────────────────┐ │ User Layer │ │ Business Logic │ │ Resource Pools │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ Instant Response│◄──►│ Assign from Pool│◄──►│ Pre-warmed AWS │ │ │ │ │ │ Resources │ └─────────────────┘ └─────────────────┘ └─────────────────┘ │ ┌───────▼────────┐ │ Background │ │ Reconciliation │ │ Workers │ └────────────────┘
The Three-Layer Pool System
Layer 1: OpenSearch Index Pool
Layer 2: AWS Knowledge Base Pool
Layer 3: User Assignment Layer
The State Machine That Changes Everything Each resource follows a deterministic state flow:PROVISIONING → AVAILABLE → ASSIGNED → CLEANUP → DESTROYED ↓ ↑ FAILED (Reconciliation) This simple state machine eliminates the complexity of managing partial states and failed operations.Each resource follows a clear state machine:: Resource is being created: Ready to be assigned to users: Currently in use by a user: Marked for deletion: Deleted (soft delete): Provisioning failed, needs cleanupThis approach provides several key benefits: is provision manager which handles the provisioning of Opensearch Indexes in the collections.
Property and Instantiation Let's create a which describes the provision manager and its property and let's try to have constructor which is utility to instantiate it.The is a custom dependency which is being injected for handling AWS Operations. The dependency which handles database operations, folks who would be familiar with would have an easy day to understand about this.It is a method of and it basically creates a new index in Opensearch. is utility function that actually generates unique name for indexes. is database operation function, which basically creates an Opensearch Index in the database with status.CreateIndexAndWaitForReady is AWS operation function which actually creates an index in Opensearch and make sure it has been created.
if the remote operation got failed, then we actually marked the state of which was created in initially in database to using function UpdateOpensearchIndexStatus function just marks the state of initially created index to such that its available to be assigned and also fill up column that justify warming of Opensearch Indexes.using database query we count indexes in 'AVAILABLE' status and aren't warming up. What I meant by warming up is that it usually takes 15 to 20 seconds for searchable, hence we consider column too for that, so for that query would be something like this (basically defined as method )using database query we also count warming available indexes using following query.using database query we also count provisioning indexes using following query.We are calculating total pool of indexes by adding all the counts, we are kind of conscious about over-provisioning hence we took this step.If calculated pool is greater than minimum size than we return.We then calculated the needed number of indexesTo simply handle the concurrency in golang we are using because,if any goroutine in the group returns an error, the entire group is considered failed, and we can handle the error appropriately.if one goroutines fails, context get cancelled, signalling other goroutines to stop their work early. method block until all goroutines complete. We create a grouping contexted using errgroup.WithContext(ctx) additionally we also limit the concurrency using method.We run loop until the needed mark is met, and we start goroutine for each count and each goroutine executed function.And we wait for all goroutine to complete using method. And then we calculate the final count for indexes and we make sure its above the minimum pool size.We would be reconciling and maintaining the pool of indexes periodically, but there would be instances where we need to trigger sometimes, hence you notice in we created a buffered channel of size 1 and its for better communication and triggering, and the size describes that its not for subsequent triggering but just we balance out with manual triggering and periodic reconciliation. does that. We have worker which periodically hit the function so basically by listening on ticker channel.Similarly for manual triggering we are listening on channel which we initialize earlier, listen on that.We have query which basically runs to claim indexes based on database state the reason we have kept is because we are choosing randomly from record we fetched based on conditions, if its locked we moved on to next record to select.And once we fetch that record, we mark it as assigned within the transaction using following query. So this is whole transaction defined as After claiming index from database we trigger manually reconciliation of indexes. We are basically handling remote index cleanup in Opensearch using method.In we first fetch the indexes for cleanup and query is like this: - We are doing joins because we wanted to make sure that indexes which are set for `CLEANUP` do have destroyed knowledge base other wise we would have cascading inconsistency, of course it would not be enforced by database, but we need to take care of that as we are working with soft-deletes which is actually a logical layer of deletion. After fetching we trying to concurrently trying to cleanup remotely and once cleaned up remotely we try to soft-delete in database using this query
CRON based Cleanup Worker According to our requirements we are never going to run this application multiple instances, hence a single instance would run and we would try to initially do vertical scaling, because we are in kind of beta mode so not expecting much traffic hence we are setting CRON job within application to schedule Cleanup, but it would be drawback and not suitable if we are ran multiple instances of this application because then coordination would be complex and that would be another day problem to solve for. We define method to run Index Manager wrapped in method We start in background as goroutine the and .Let's define Knowledge base Manager Initiating Knowledge base manager
Provision New Knowledge Base The reason we are injecting index manager into knowledge base manager because we need to claim indexes for knowledge bases Initially we are claiming indexes from index managerThen we create AWS Knowledge base in our database using following query, The we create knowledge base actually in AWS, and if something fails we handle rollback by marking index for cleanup and delete the initiated state in database.Then after creating knowledge base we need to create data source and then we finalize the knowledge base creation. again we handle rollback if finalization failed by deleting remote data sources, knowledge bases and marking opensearch indexes for cleanup.
Reconciling Knowledge Base Pool Similar to what we do in index manager for reconciliation of pool we do the same for knowledge baseThe only difference is we don't have warming instances for this. Then whenever whatever components where knowledge base manager will be utilize they would be able to get available knowledge base using following method. And we are triggering for reconciliation similar to index manager
Cleaning up Knowledge Bases Similar to the index manager we cleanup remote resources, we mark Opensearch Index in Index Manager for cleanup and then delete the state in database for Knowledge base.We also setup cron job for the same
Running Knowledge base manager As background goroutine we start reconciliation worker and cleanup worker.Prime pooling is something very integral part of this orchestration drama because for the first time when application is running it won't have anything no pre-provision remote indexes or knowledge bases, so we wanted to initially pre-provision minimum pool requirement for those resources and then start the application.We define method for that.We are having local context whose expiry is 60 minutes which is enough for this.And we make sure all the indexes and knowledge bases are ready and not just warming up.
Symphony - How it all come together to play out. I have provided you that's the entry point of application and this is how we handle thingsWe are creating context which gets canceled automatically when OS signals like pressing Ctrl + C (syscall.SIGINT), when systems sends terminating signal (syscall.SIGTERM).Then we are creating logging object based on zerologwe are loading our configuration from yaml file.creating instance of database and AWS for their corresponding operationThen we create group context for handling background goroutinesThen we initially synchronously make sure all the resources are present before starting the application using This is how I worked around pre-provision, I hope this helps.
